metadata.title: Q9(R1) Quality Risk Management — Guidance for Industry
metadata.document_version: Q9(R1) — ICH Step 4, January 2023; FDA adoption May 2023
metadata.extraction_note: IMPORTANT: The filename '33-master-protocols.pdf' is a mislabeled file. The actual document is ICH Q9(R1) Quality Risk Management, May 2023, issued by FDA/CDER/CBER as an ICH-Quality guidance. It covers pharmaceutical quality risk management principles, processes, methodology, and integration into industry and regulatory operations. No master protocols content is present. Rules extracted reflect the actual Q9(R1) document content. Rule IDs use topic abbreviation QRM (Quality Risk Management).

rules.rule_text: Quality risk management must be based on scientific knowledge and must ultimately link to the protection of the patient, including situations where product availability may be impacted.
rules.source_section: Section III — Principles of Quality Risk Management (3)
rules.verbatim_quote: The evaluation of the risk to quality should be based on scientific knowledge and ultimately link to the protection of the patient. (Note: Risk to quality includes situations where product availability may be impacted, leading to potential patient harm.)

rules.rule_text: The level of effort, formality, and documentation of the quality risk management process must be commensurate with the level of risk.
rules.source_section: Section III — Principles of Quality Risk Management (3)
rules.verbatim_quote: The level of effort, formality and documentation of the quality risk management process should be commensurate with the level of risk.

rules.rule_text: Quality risk management must incorporate all components of the framework — risk assessment, risk control, risk communication, and risk review — at a level of detail commensurate with the specific risk.
rules.source_section: Section IV — General Quality Risk Management Process (4)
rules.verbatim_quote: Quality risk management is a systematic process for the assessment, control, communication, and review of risks to the quality of the drug (medicinal) product across the product lifecycle... a robust process will incorporate consideration of all the elements at a level of detail that is commensurate with the specific risk.
rules.exceptions: Emphasis on each component may differ case to case.

rules.rule_text: Decision makers must take responsibility for coordinating quality risk management across various functions and departments of their organization, assure that a QRM process is defined, deployed, and reviewed with adequate resources and knowledge, and assure that subjectivity is managed and minimized.
rules.source_section: Section IV.A — Responsibilities (4.1)
rules.verbatim_quote: Decision makers should: Take responsibility for coordinating quality risk management across various functions and departments of their organization; Assure that a quality risk management process is defined, deployed, and reviewed and that adequate resources and knowledge are available; and Assure that subjectivity in quality risk management activities is managed and minimized, to facilitate scientifically robust risk-based decision-making

rules.rule_text: Quality risk management teams must include experts from appropriate areas, including quality unit, product development, engineering, regulatory affairs, production operations, and individuals knowledgeable about the QRM process.
rules.source_section: Section IV.A — Responsibilities (4.1)
rules.verbatim_quote: When teams are formed, they should include experts from the appropriate areas (e.g., quality unit, product development, business development, engineering, regulatory affairs, production operations, sales and marketing, supply chain, legal, statistics and clinical) in addition to individuals who are knowledgeable about the quality risk management process.
rules.exceptions: Quality risk management activities are usually, but not always, undertaken by interdisciplinary teams.

rules.rule_text: Initiating a quality risk management process must include: defining the problem/risk question with pertinent assumptions, assembling background data on potential hazards, identifying a leader and resources, and specifying a timeline, deliverables, and appropriate level of decision-making.
rules.source_section: Section IV.B — Initiating a Quality Risk Management Process (4.2)
rules.verbatim_quote: Possible steps used to initiate and plan a quality risk management process might include the following: Define the problem and/or risk question, including pertinent assumptions identifying the potential for risk; Assemble background information and/or data on the potential hazard, harm, or human health impact relevant to the risk assessment; Identify a leader and necessary resources; Specify a timeline, deliverables, and appropriate level of decision-making for the risk management process

rules.rule_text: Risk assessment must consist of hazard identification, risk analysis, and risk evaluation, beginning with a well-defined problem description or risk question.
rules.source_section: Section IV.C — Risk Assessment (4.3)
rules.verbatim_quote: Risk assessment consists of the identification of hazards and the analysis and evaluation of risks associated with exposure to those hazards (as defined below). Quality risk assessments begin with a well-defined problem description or risk question.

rules.rule_text: Risk analysis must estimate risk by linking the likelihood of occurrence and severity of harm; detectability may also factor into risk estimation.
rules.source_section: Section IV.C — Risk Assessment (4.3)
rules.verbatim_quote: Risk analysis is the estimation of the risk associated with the identified hazards. It is the qualitative or quantitative process of linking the likelihood of occurrence and severity of harms. In some risk management tools, the ability to detect the harm (detectability) also factors in the estimation of risk.
rules.exceptions: Detectability is not a discrete factor in all quality risk management methods.

rules.rule_text: Risk evaluation must compare identified and analyzed risks against given risk criteria, considering the strength of evidence for all three fundamental questions. Assumptions and sources of uncertainty must be revealed to enhance confidence in the output.
rules.source_section: Section IV.C — Risk Assessment (4.3)
rules.verbatim_quote: Risk evaluation compares the identified and analyzed risk against given risk criteria. Risk evaluations consider the strength of evidence for all three of the fundamental questions... Revealing assumptions and reasonable sources of uncertainty will enhance confidence in this output and/or help identify its limitations.

rules.rule_text: The purpose of risk control is to reduce risk to an acceptable level. The amount of effort used for risk control must be proportional to the significance of the risk.
rules.source_section: Section IV.D — Risk Control (4.4)
rules.verbatim_quote: Risk control includes decision-making to reduce and/or accept risks. The purpose of risk control is to reduce the risk to an acceptable level. The amount of effort used for risk control should be proportional to the significance of the risk.

rules.rule_text: After implementing risk reduction measures, the risk assessment must be revisited to identify and evaluate any possible change in risk, because risk reduction measures can introduce new risks or increase significance of other existing risks.
rules.source_section: Section IV.D — Risk Control (4.4)
rules.verbatim_quote: The implementation of risk reduction measures can introduce new risks into the system or increase the significance of other existing risks. Hence, it might be appropriate to revisit the risk assessment to identify and evaluate any possible change in risk after implementing a risk reduction process.

rules.rule_text: The output/result of the quality risk management process must be appropriately communicated and documented.
rules.source_section: Section IV.E — Risk Communication (4.5)
rules.verbatim_quote: The output/result of the quality risk management process should be appropriately communicated and documented (see Fig. 1: solid arrows).
rules.exceptions: Communication need not be carried out for each and every risk acceptance.

rules.rule_text: Between industry and regulatory authorities, communication concerning quality risk management decisions must be conducted through existing channels as specified in regulations and guidances.
rules.source_section: Section IV.E — Risk Communication (4.5)
rules.verbatim_quote: Between the industry and regulatory authorities, communication concerning quality risk management decisions might be conducted through existing channels as specified in regulations and guidances.

rules.rule_text: Risk management must be an ongoing part of the quality management process. A mechanism to review or monitor events must be implemented, and results reviewed to take into account new knowledge and experience.
rules.source_section: Section IV.F — Risk Review (4.6)
rules.verbatim_quote: Risk management should be an ongoing part of the quality management process. A mechanism to review or monitor events should be implemented. The output/results of the risk management process should be reviewed to take into account new knowledge and experience.

rules.rule_text: The frequency of any risk review must be based upon the level of risk and should continue to be utilized for events that might impact the original QRM decision, whether planned or unplanned.
rules.source_section: Section IV.F — Risk Review (4.6)
rules.verbatim_quote: Once a quality risk management process has been initiated, that process should continue to be utilized for events that might impact the original quality risk management decision, whether these events are planned (e.g., results of product review, inspections, audits, change control) or unplanned (e.g., root cause from failure investigations, recall). The frequency of any review should be based upon the level of risk.

rules.rule_text: Resource constraints must not be used to justify the use of lower levels of formality in the quality risk management process. Risk scores, ratings, and assessments must be based on appropriate use of evidence, science, and knowledge.
rules.source_section: Section V.A — Formality in Quality Risk Management (5.1)
rules.verbatim_quote: Resource constraints should not be used to justify the use of lower levels of formality in the quality risk management process. Risk scores, ratings and assessments should be based on an appropriate use of evidence, science, and knowledge. Regardless of how much formality is applied, the robust management of risk is the goal of the process.

rules.rule_text: The overall approach for determining how much formality to apply during QRM activities must be described within the quality system. Higher uncertainty, importance, or complexity may require more formal QRM approaches.
rules.source_section: Section V.A — Formality in Quality Risk Management (5.1)
rules.verbatim_quote: The overall approach for determining how much formality to apply during quality risk management activities should be described within the quality system... Higher levels of uncertainty, importance or complexity may require more formal quality risk management approaches to manage potential risks and to support effective risk-based decision-making.

rules.rule_text: It is important to ensure the integrity of the data that are used for risk-based decision-making, as all decision-making relies on the use of knowledge.
rules.source_section: Section V.B — Risk-Based Decision-Making (5.2)
rules.verbatim_quote: As all decision-making relies on the use of knowledge, see ICH Q10 for guidance in relation to knowledge management. It is important also to ensure the integrity of the data that are used for risk-based decision-making.

rules.rule_text: All participants involved with quality risk management activities must acknowledge, anticipate, and address the potential for subjectivity.
rules.source_section: Section V.C — Managing and Minimizing Subjectivity (5.3)
rules.verbatim_quote: All participants involved with quality risk management activities should acknowledge, anticipate, and address the potential for subjectivity.
rules.exceptions: While subjectivity cannot be completely eliminated from quality risk management activities, it may be controlled by addressing bias and assumptions, the proper use of quality risk management tools and maximizing the use of relevant data and sources of knowledge.

rules.rule_text: Appropriate use of quality risk management facilitates but does not obviate industry's obligation to comply with regulatory requirements and does not replace appropriate communications between industry and regulators.
rules.source_section: Section I — Introduction (1)
rules.verbatim_quote: Appropriate use of quality risk management can facilitate but does not obviate industry's obligation to comply with regulatory requirements and does not replace appropriate communications between industry and regulators. Quality risk management should not be used in a manner where decisions are made that justify a practice that would otherwise, in accordance with regulations and/or guidance, be deemed unacceptable.

rules.rule_text: Quality/manufacturing issues, including non-compliance with GMP, must be addressed through risk-based drug shortage prevention and mitigation activities that proactively manage supply chain complexities and ensure availability of needed drug products.
rules.source_section: Section VI.A — The Role of QRM in Addressing Product Availability Risks (6.1)
rules.verbatim_quote: Quality/manufacturing issues, including non-compliance with Good Manufacturing Practice (GMP), are a significant cause of product availability issues (e.g., product shortages). The interests of patients are served by risk-based drug shortage prevention and mitigation activities that help to proactively manage supply chain complexities and ensure availability of needed drug (medicinal) products.

rules.rule_text: The pharmaceutical quality system must support effective oversight and response to evolving quality/manufacturing risks from the pharmaceutical company or its external partners through use of quality risk management and knowledge management as an early warning system.
rules.source_section: Section VI.A — The Role of QRM in Addressing Product Availability Risks (6.1)
rules.verbatim_quote: The pharmaceutical quality system, including management responsibilities, also uses quality risk management and knowledge management to provide an early warning system that supports effective oversight and response to evolving quality/manufacturing risks from the pharmaceutical company or its external partners.

rules.rule_text: Approval and oversight of outsourced activities and material suppliers must be informed by risk assessments, effective knowledge management, and an effective monitoring strategy for supply chain partner performance.
rules.source_section: Section VI.A(c) — Oversight of Outsourced Activities and Suppliers
rules.verbatim_quote: Approval and oversight of outsourced activities and material suppliers is informed by risk assessments, effective knowledge management, and an effective monitoring strategy for supply chain partner performance... When substantial variability is identified in the quality and safety of supplied materials or in the services provided, enhanced review and monitoring activities are justified.
rules.exceptions: In some cases, it may be necessary to identify a new supply chain entity (e.g., a pre-qualified alternative option) to perform a function.

rules.rule_text: QRM must be applied to the design, validation, and technology transfer of advanced production processes and analytical methods, advanced data analysis methods, and computerized systems when applying digitalization and emerging technologies.
rules.source_section: Section I — Introduction (1)
rules.verbatim_quote: The application of quality risk management to the design, validation and technology transfer of advanced production processes and analytical methods, advanced data analysis methods and computerized systems is important.

rules.rule_text: FMEA must rely on product and process understanding to evaluate potential failure modes for processes and their likely effect on outcomes and/or product performance; once established, risk reduction can be used to eliminate, contain, reduce, or control potential failures.
rules.source_section: Annex I — Section I.2, Failure Mode Effects Analysis (FMEA)
rules.verbatim_quote: FMEA (see IEC 60812) provides for an evaluation of potential failure modes for processes and their likely effect on outcomes and/or product performance. Once failure modes are established, risk reduction can be used to eliminate, contain, reduce, or control the potential failures. FMEA relies on product and process understanding.

rules.rule_text: HACCP implementation must follow a seven-step process including: hazard analysis, determining critical control points, establishing critical limits, establishing a monitoring system, establishing corrective actions, establishing a verification system, and establishing a record-keeping system.
rules.source_section: Annex I — Section I.5, Hazard Analysis and Critical Control Points (HACCP)
rules.verbatim_quote: HACCP consists of the following seven steps: 1. Conduct a hazard analysis and identify preventive measures for each step of the process; 2. Determine the critical control points; 3. Establish critical limits; 4. Establish a system to monitor the critical control points; 5. Establish the corrective action to be taken when monitoring indicates that the critical control points are not in a state of control; 6. Establish system to verify that the HACCP system is working effectively; 7. Establish a record-keeping system
rules.exceptions: HACCP is most useful when product and process understanding is sufficiently comprehensive to support identification of critical control points.
cross_references: WHO Technical Report Series No 908, 2003 Annex 7

rules.rule_text: QRM must be applied as part of change management to evaluate the impact of changes on product quality and on the availability of the final product, and to determine appropriate actions preceding the implementation of a change.
rules.source_section: Annex II.A — Quality Risk Management as Part of Integrated Quality Management Documentation (II.1)
rules.verbatim_quote: To manage changes based on knowledge and information accumulated in pharmaceutical development and during manufacturing; To evaluate the impact of the changes on the availability of the final product; To evaluate the impact on product quality of changes to the facility, equipment, material, manufacturing process or technical transfers; To determine appropriate actions preceding the implementation of a change, e.g., additional testing, (re)qualification, (re)validation or communication with regulators.

rules.rule_text: QRM must be applied to label control procedures based on the potential for mix-ups involving different product labels, including different versions of the same label.
rules.source_section: Annex II.H — Quality Risk Management as Part of Packaging and Labeling Design of Packages (II.8)
rules.verbatim_quote: To design label control procedures based on the potential for mix-ups involving different product labels, including different versions of the same label.

rules.rule_text: QRM must be applied to identify the scope and extent of verification, qualification, and validation activities and to distinguish between critical and non-critical process steps to facilitate design of a validation study.
rules.source_section: Annex II.F — Quality Risk Management as Part of Production Validation (II.6)
rules.verbatim_quote: To identify the scope and extent of verification, qualification, and validation activities (e.g., analytical methods, processes, equipment, and cleaning methods); To determine the extent for follow-up activities (e.g., sampling, monitoring and re-validation); To distinguish between critical and non-critical process steps to facilitate design of a validation study.

rules.rule_text: Regarding supply chain control, product lifecycle oversight must include maintaining current knowledge of quality/manufacturing hazards and prioritizing efforts to manage such risks; understanding these hazards is critical to maintaining supply predictability.
rules.source_section: Annex II.I — Quality Risk Management as Part of Supply Chain Control (II.9)
rules.verbatim_quote: Regarding product availability risks related to quality/manufacturing issues, product lifecycle oversight of the supply chain includes maintaining current knowledge of quality/manufacturing hazards and prioritizing efforts to manage such risks. Understanding hazards to quality/manufacturing is critical to maintaining supply predictability. When risks are well understood and controlled, a higher confidence in product availability can be attained.
